questions.question: Which relational operator tests whether two values are NOT equal?
questions.explanation: The != operator tests for inequality in C++. The == operator tests for equality. <> and !== are not valid C++ operators.
The logical AND (&&) operator requires both conditions to be true. Since 5 > 3 is true and 5 < 10 is true, the overall expression is true, so "Yes" is printed.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the break statement inside a switch case?
choices: It exits the switch statement so subsequent cases are not executed
It skips to the next case
It restarts the switch from the beginning
questions.explanation: Without break, execution falls through to the next case. The break statement exits the switch block, preventing fall-through to subsequent cases.

questions.question: What does the conditional (ternary) operator expression (x > 0) ? "positive" : "non-positive" evaluate to when x is -3?
questions.explanation: The conditional operator ?: evaluates the condition first. Since -3 > 0 is false, the expression after the colon is returned: "non-positive".

questions.question: The logical ___ operator (!) reverses the truth or falsity of a condition.
questions.explanation: The logical NOT operator (!) negates a Boolean expression. If a condition is true, !condition is false, and vice versa.

questions.question: In a switch statement, the ___ keyword handles any value not matched by a case label.
questions.explanation: The default case in a switch statement executes when none of the case values match the switch expression. It is optional but recommended.
